summary: I was born in Moos Manj, Laos, in 1947. After the communist takeover of Laos, my family and I could no longer stay there, so we fled across the Mekong River seeking safety. Laos is where my early life and family roots are, but the war forced us to leave everything behind.

The Mekong River was the dangerous boundary we had to cross to escape Laos and reach Thailand. I carried my children across it, using a baby carrier to keep them safe, but the river's strong current was terrifying. Sadly, during the crossing, I lost one of my babies in the river, and despite searching, we never found him.

After fleeing Laos, we crossed the Mekong River into Thailand, hoping for safety. We first stayed near the riverbank and later moved to places like Nong Khai and the Ban Vinai refugee camp. Thailand became a temporary home for us during those difficult years before we eventually emigrated to the United States in 1988.

Nong Khai was one of the places in Thailand where I lived after escaping Laos. It was farther from the riverbank where we crossed, and it provided some distance from the dangers we faced. Living there was part of our journey as refugees before moving to the Ban Vinai camp and eventually to America.

I lived in the Ban Vinai refugee camp in Thailand for eight years after fleeing Laos. During that time, I gave birth to three more children there. The camp was a difficult but necessary place for us as we waited for the chance to start a new life elsewhere, which eventually happened when we moved to the United States in 1988.

In 1988, I emigrated to the United States, arriving on September 15. It was a new beginning after years of hardship in Laos and Thailand. Since coming here, I have had five more children, and this country has become my home where I have built my family and life.

I am part of the Hmong people, who have faced many struggles, especially during the war in Laos. Our traditions and beliefs, like those about spirits and signs, have helped us endure hardships. My family's journey from Laos to Thailand and then to the United States reflects the resilience of the Hmong community.

My name is Yeng Xiong, born in 1947 in Moos Manj, Laos. I lived through the communist takeover, fled across the Mekong River with my family, and endured the loss of one of my babies during that escape. After years in refugee camps in Thailand, I emigrated to the United States in 1988, where I have raised my family and kept the memory of my lost child close to my heart.
